*   A man in his twenties, also a university student, was arrested in Shah Alam, Malaysia after CCTV footage went viral showing him repeatedly stalking a 22-year-old female student and sniffing her shoes outside her flat.
*   The victim filed a police report alleging the suspect had harassed her since April both at her university and her residence. The suspect, who had no prior criminal record, was remanded for one day for further investigation.
*   The incident tests Malaysia's anti-stalking law (Section 507A of the Penal Code), which criminalised stalking in 2023. Offenders face up to three years in prison or a fine.
*   The law's first major case involved photographer Acacia Diana, whose eight-year stalker was acquitted in 2024 due to insanity and ordered to a psychiatric hospital.
*   A 2020 study found 88% of 1,008 Malaysians surveyed had experienced stalking-related behaviour, with over a third reporting fear-inducing incidents.

articles.title: Why tech titans’ IPOs matter as much in China as in the US
articles.summary:
- SpaceX's potential US$75 billion IPO would be the largest in history, shattering Saudi Aramco's record and potentially valuing the company at US$1.75 trillion.
- Anthropic's valuation surged to US$965 billion from US$380 billion in February after a US$65 billion funding round, with a revenue run rate hitting US$47 billion in May.
- OpenAI, with 900 million weekly active users for ChatGPT, has an estimated US$852 billion valuation and is also preparing for a potential IPO.
- Collectively, these three tech IPOs could add nearly US$4 trillion in market capitalization to US exchanges.
- This wave of mega-IPOs intensifies global competition for capital, putting further strain on foreign investment restrictions and capital flows into China.

articles.title: Over half of tested reading glasses fail to list key details to prevent eye strain
articles.summary:
*   Hong Kong's Consumer Council tested 17 models of pre-made reading glasses (+2.50 dioptres), finding that 10 models lacked the mandatory labeling of pupillary distance, a key measurement for proper alignment and avoiding eye strain.
*   While all models passed durability and safety tests, only seven models even indicated pupillary distance. Of those, only three met European standards for prismatic power tolerance; the other four had deviations that could cause visual discomfort.
*   The council warned that incorrect pupillary distance forces eye muscles to compensate, potentially leading to fatigue, headaches, and double vision.
*   Prices ranged widely from HK$8 to HK$490, and only seven models provided necessary instructions or warnings to consumers.
*   The watchdog urged manufacturers to improve transparency and accuracy of product information to help consumers make suitable choices.

articles.title: China’s Xi Jinping arrives in a North Korea armed with advanced warships and AI drones
articles.summary:
*   North Korea has launched the *Choe Hyon*-class destroyer, its largest surface combatant at 5,000 tonnes displacement, featuring phased-array radar and vertical launch systems potentially for hypersonic and nuclear-capable missiles, with technical support from Russia.
*   Pyongyang is constructing its first nuclear-powered strategic submarine with a reported displacement of 8,700 tonnes, which would significantly enhance underwater operational endurance and complicate regional missile defense.
*   Kim Jong-un has mandated the rapid strengthening of naval forces for nuclear deterrence, ordering the deployment of two additional destroyers per year, with the third reportedly under construction.
*   North Korean forces have gained practical combat experience in unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) warfare through their participation in the war in Ukraine, while also building their own military drone capabilities.

articles.title: How Hong Kong can draw line under the 2019-20 protests and move forward
articles.summary:
*   A total of 10,286 people were arrested in relation to the 2019-20 protests, but only 2,978 have been formally charged, leaving over 7,000 in a state of legal uncertainty.
*   Those not charged remain on bail under the Police Force Ordinance, facing restrictions such as reporting requirements, sureties, and potential travel bans.
*   The prolonged legal status creates significant indirect social and economic impacts for the individuals, their families, and the community at large, affecting life decisions like marriage, careers, and business.
*   Secretary for Security Chris Tang suggested that showing remorse could lead to earlier case reviews, but the practical criteria and potential benefits of this process are undefined and unclear.

articles.title: 2 arrested over viral video row involving off-duty Hong Kong firefighter
articles.summary:
*   An off-duty Hong Kong firefighter was arrested for common assault after a viral video showed him shouting at and shoving a young couple in the Sim City shopping centre, Mong Kok.
*   Police made a second arrest of a 33-year-old man suspected of bumping into the firefighter's wife, which sources indicated was the initial trigger for the dispute.
*   The incident occurred on a Saturday, and the subsequent 24-second video posted on Instagram sparked significant online backlash, attracting over 1,000 comments.
*   The Fire Services Department confirmed the arrested 48-year-old man is its employee. Both men were arrested on suspicion of common assault.

articles.title: Where do office supplies belong? Here’s a hint: it’s in the name
articles.summary:
• Office supplies are the property of the employer, and using them for personal use (e.g., taking them home) without specific consent constitutes theft.  
• While the value may be small, the legal principle is the same as stealing larger amounts, as it involves taking another's property dishonestly.  
• Under employment law, such theft is a valid reason for immediate dismissal due to dishonest conduct, which can severely impact future employment prospects.  
• The act reflects on individual respect for rules and broader societal civility, aligning with the "broken windows" theory where minor infractions indicate larger issues.

articles.title: China sends a message to Pacific islands amid unease over missile test
articles.summary:
*   China conducted its first strategic submarine-launched ballistic missile test into international waters in a decade on July 6, drawing protests from several Pacific island nations, including the Solomon Islands.
*   Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi reaffirmed "unconditional support" for the Solomon Islands and pledged to deepen cooperation, emphasizing that China's engagement has "no geopolitical intent."
*   Solomon Islands Prime Minister Matthew Wale lodged a "strong protest" over the missile test, stating, "Be our friend, but don't threaten us," reflecting regional unease.
*   Wang Yi defended China's role in the region, asserting it does not seek "spheres of influence" and that island states are sovereign, "not anyone's backyard."

articles.title: Will AI kill English classes? Chinese university to drop them as tech booms
articles.summary:
- Shenzhen University of Advanced Technology (SUAT) will phase out traditional English courses starting in 2026, replacing them with intercultural communication classes, citing advances in real-time AI translation as sufficient for language bridging.
- The university's provost, Zhao Wei, stated that AI translation tools are now effective enough to eliminate the need for direct language learning, shifting focus to skills AI cannot provide, such as human judgment, empathy, and cross-cultural etiquette.
- This move reflects a broader debate within China's higher education sector on reforming foreign-language education in the AI era, with experts advocating for a transition from pure language instruction to models emphasizing intercultural competence, critical thinking, and AI literacy.
- The approach aligns with China's national emphasis on bolstering cultural confidence and soft power, with some suggesting the integration of Chinese classics into language courses to enhance international communication abilities.

articles.title: CUHK academic who allegedly hid marital status from woman denies indecent assault
articles.summary:
- Peng Peng, an associate professor at the Chinese University of Hong Kong, pleaded not guilty to two counts of indecent assault against a female scholar at Sha Tin Court.
- The complainant alleges Peng touched her inappropriately on May 21, 2025; the defense argues their interactions were consensual and suggests she was motivated by anger after learning he was married.
- The complainant reported the alleged assaults to police shortly after discovering Peng's marital status, which he had not disclosed during their previous meetings.
- The court has reserved its verdict for August 24.

articles.title: 80 years on from WWII, China’s interests lie in peaceful Asia-Pacific: Rana Mitter
articles.summary:
- World War II was a defining trauma for China, causing millions of deaths, mass displacement, and the destruction of infrastructure built in the 1920s-30s, forcing the country to rebuild from a devastated state.
- The war's conditions made a revolution more likely than reform by creating extreme economic and social hardship, which allowed the Chinese Communist Party to expand its influence, particularly in rural areas.
- Many foundational aspects of modern China, including the "work unit" system and an expanded public role for women in national defense and health, emerged or solidified during the wartime period.
- The 80th anniversary presents an opportunity for China to improve diplomacy, but its approach is often hindered by a focus on historical grievances rather than framing engagement in forward-looking, positive terms.

articles.title: China’s second-quarter GDP growth misses mark, with lowest reading since end of 2022
articles.summary:
*   China's GDP grew by 4.3% in Q2 2026, missing economists' forecasts and marking the slowest expansion since the end of 2022. This decelerated from the 5% growth in Q1, bringing first-half growth to 4.7%.
*   Exports remained a strong point, rising 27% year-on-year in June and 17.6% for the first half, demonstrating resilience despite global disruptions and underscoring a deepening divergence between external and domestic demand.
*   Domestic demand showed persistent weakness. Fixed-asset investment fell by 5.7% year-on-year in the first half, while retail sales saw a modest 1% year-on-year increase in June after a decline in May.
*   Officials expressed confidence in meeting the full-year target of 4.5-5% growth, but analysts noted that without swifter policy action, the recent stabilization in consumption may not be sustained.

articles.title: A Trump visit to Hong Kong would be a strategic masterstroke
articles.summary:
*   Hong Kong has made a strong economic comeback, overtaking Switzerland as the world's largest cross-border wealth center with US$2.95 trillion in offshore assets and rising to 2nd place in global competitiveness rankings.
*   Geopolitical tensions, such as Chinese companies delisting from the US, have inadvertently boosted Hong Kong's role as a financial hub, attracting talent and reinforcing its position as an East-West bridge.
*   Improved US-China relations, following the Trump-Xi summit, create a strategic opportunity for Hong Kong to repair ties with the US and Western allies after years of strain caused by sanctions and reduced official contact.
*   Since 2020, official engagement between Hong Kong and foreign leaders, especially the US, has sharply declined, hindering the city's ability to project soft power and international stature.

articles.title: China’s StepFun claims it has unveiled the world’s first AI smartphone
articles.summary:
Here are the key points from the article:

*   **Product Launch:** Chinese AI startup StepFun, backed by Tencent, has announced the "StepX Neo," claiming it to be the world's first "agentic smartphone."
*   **Core Technology:** The device runs on a new operating system called Step AOS, designed specifically for AI agents rather than being an add-on to existing mobile software. It features a native AI assistant named "Amoo."
*   **Functionality:** Unlike traditional phones, it is built to interpret requests, coordinate across multiple integrated Chinese digital services (e.g., Alipay, Didi, Meituan), and autonomously execute complex tasks without user intervention.
*   **Business Model & Strategy:** StepFun aims to pioneer an independent, AI-native ecosystem. The company states it will not rely on traditional hardware sales or app fees, focusing instead on exploring new monetization models for AI devices.
*   **Industry Context & Analysis:** This launch reflects a global trend

articles.title: Wu Shicun on why Beijing will not rule out Scarborough Shoal construction
articles.summary:
- Wu Shicun states that the Philippines' "assertive transparency" campaign involves deliberately provoking confrontations at Renai Jiao (Second Thomas Shaal) and then using onboard media to create viral videos that frame China as the aggressor, aiming to secure budget and equipment support from its Congress.
- In contrast, China's primary diplomatic focus is on rule-making and institutional frameworks, such as promoting consultations for a South China Sea Code of Conduct (COC) since 2013 and participating in regular working group meetings to implement the Declaration on the Conduct of Parties (DOC), with the 56th session recently held in Vietnam.
- China has undergone a "paradigm shift" in its narrative strategy, moving from a passive position to proactively countering and exposing the Philippines' tactics, a change learned from recent disputes. Wu also indicates that Beijing does not rule out future construction at Scarborough Shoal.

- The move aligns with Beijing's recent push for "patient capital," a long-term, risk-tolerant investment strategy emphasized by the Communist Party's theoretical journal, *Qiushi*, to support strategic industries amid global competition.
- Jiangxi provincial government-backed Jiangxi Ganyue Expressway also announced a 200 million yuan semiconductor fund, focusing on domestic chipmaker

articles.title: Long March-10B: China’s net-capture rocket joins space race with edge on SpaceX
articles.summary:
- China successfully achieved controlled recovery of an orbital-class rocket booster for the first time, using the Long March-10B's maiden flight to deploy a satellite and vertically land the first stage via a sea-based net-capture system.
- This makes China only the second nation, after the U.S. with SpaceX's Falcon 9, to master controlled rocket booster recovery, utilizing a distinct sea-based net-and-cable method instead of land-based legs.
- The Long March-10B is a medium-lift, two-stage rocket (63 meters tall) powered by kerosene-liquid oxygen engines, capable of delivering 16 tonnes to low Earth orbit, and is designed primarily for commercial cargo missions like satellite internet constellations.
- The mission validates reusable rocket design for China, aiming to significantly cut launch costs, increase launch frequency, and strengthen the commercial space sector amid a global race to the moon.
- This success follows two prior failed attempts by other Chinese rockets to achieve SpaceX-style vertical landings in December, marking a critical advance in China's path to reusability and space competitiveness.

articles.title: EU signals emergency import curbs after dramatic growth in Chinese trade gap
articles.summary:
- The EU is prepared to introduce emergency safeguard measures, including tariffs and quotas, on a case-by-case basis to combat a dramatic surge in Chinese imports.
- The EU's trade deficit with China grew by 23.7% in the first half of the year, with Germany's surplus with China expanding by 80.8%.
- The EU anticipates China's share of global manufacturing will rise to 45% by 2030, describing this as a significant and concerning form of industrial dominance.
- EU officials stated they have little expectation China will make structural economic changes to reduce the trade imbalance before a warning deadline in October.
- China has reportedly offered to make purchase commitments to reduce the trade deficit, particularly through government procurement channels.

articles.title: China report warns against European-led Nato 3.0 expansion in the Asia-Pacific
articles.summary:
*   The report from China's CICIR think tank argues that NATO's transition to a more Europe-led "NATO 3.0" model will fail to resolve fundamental strategic rifts between the US and Europe, as their long-term goals diverge.
*   The shift is driven by US pressure for increased European burden-sharing, but the US aims to reduce costs and maintain its influence over procurement, while Europe seeks genuine strategic autonomy and a stronger independent defense industry.
*   CICIR warns that NATO's expanding military cooperation with Asia-Pacific nations like Japan and South Korea is increasingly out of step with a developing multipolar world order.
*   Beijing views this deepening footprint in Asia as part of a US-led effort to contain China, potentially building an "Asian version of NATO."

articles.title: China warns of more extreme weather as Bavi leaves trail of rain, flooding
articles.summary:
- **Immediate Impact:** Former typhoon Bavi caused heavy rain and flooding in Northeast China, prompting emergency alerts, evacuations, and flood controls in Jilin province. Landslide risks were reported in at least 10 cities near the Changbai Mountains.

- **Future Weather Warnings:** China's State Council forecasted 5-6 more typhoons in the region by the end of next month, with 2-3 expected to affect the country. Heavy rain and floods are anticipated through September, with some northern regions expected to receive 20-50% more rainfall than usual.

- **Record Rainfall Data:** Average rainfall in northern China for this flood season reached 22.8 cm, the highest in a decade and 23.1% above normal. Hebei and Tianjin recorded their heaviest rainfall since 1961.

- **Climate and Global Factors:** Experts attribute the increasing frequency of extreme weather to global warming and El Niño. A "heat dome" in Europe can trigger atmospheric shifts affecting China, but local factors like typhoon remnants and cold air intrusions also play a role.

- **Regional Vulnerability:** Northern China faces greater challenges due to less developed flood defenses, drainage, and public awareness compared to historically flood-prone southern areas. Authorities are urged to strengthen extreme weather preparedness.

articles.title: Hong Kong exchange marks progress in luring more listings from abroad
articles.summary:
*   Hong Kong's stock exchange is successfully attracting more foreign companies, such as Kazakhstan's state railway operator (Kazakhstan Temir Zholy) and firms from Canada and Indonesia, seeking international capital.
*   The listing pipeline includes companies like Canada's Silvercorp Metals and Indonesia's PT MNC Digital Entertainment, with accounting firm PwC forecasting an increase in international issuers in 2026-2027.
*   Kazakhstan Temir Zholy cited the Belt and Road Initiative as a reason for choosing Hong Kong; it recorded a 2023 net profit of approximately US$735 million.
*   Analysts note that companies listing before the end of 2026 face shorter windows and aim to launch when market sentiment is favorable, often before the US midterm elections.

articles.title: Delhi summons Iranian diplomat after Indian sailor dies in Strait of Hormuz vessel attacks
articles.summary:
- India summoned Iran’s deputy chief of mission to New Delhi to lodge a strong protest after attacks on two commercial vessels (MT Al Bahiyah and MT Mombasa) in the Strait of Hormuz, which killed an Indian sailor and wounded others.
- The two vessels carried a combined crew of 46, including 30 Indians; India expressed deep concern and called for an immediate halt to violence in accordance with international law.
- The attacks coincide with a renewed escalation between the United States and Iran, including recent US strikes on Tehran and heightened tensions over control of the Strait of Hormuz.
- India is a major supplier of global merchant sailors, with over 320,000 active seafarers in 2025, making its nationals particularly vulnerable in high-risk shipping lanes.
- Another incident on Sunday off the coast of Oman left one Indian sailor missing after an attack that Iran claimed was a response to an unauthorized transit attempt.

articles.title: China’s rare earth weakness, new rocket to rival SpaceX: 7 science highlights
articles.summary:
- China's Long March-10B rocket achieved a controlled recovery of its orbital-class booster, becoming only the second nation (after SpaceX) to demonstrate this reusable launch technology.
- A Chinese study identifies a critical weakness in China's dominant rare earth industry, challenging its perceived strategic advantage in mining, refining, and exports.
- China's defence scientists unveiled a high-power microwave weapon arsenal with a

articles.title: US National Science Foundation to ban projects with flagged Chinese institutions
articles.summary:
*   The U.S. National Science Foundation (NSF) will implement a new policy banning the use of its funds for collaborations with Chinese institutions and personnel on U.S. restricted-party lists.
*   The policy change reflects a congressional push to curb academic partnerships over national security concerns, aiming to prevent U.S.-funded research from aiding China's military and technological development.
*   The move marks a shift from the NSF's previous "TRUST" framework, which attempted to balance security risks with the benefits of international scientific collaboration.
*   U.S. Representative John Moolenaar praised the policy as "commendable," while a Chinese embassy spokesperson urged for more scientific exchange rather than restrictions.

articles.title: A cloud above the clouds: US, China race to make space a computing platform
articles.summary:
- The US and China are engaged in a new "space race" focused on establishing space as a computing and digital infrastructure platform, marking a shift from the Cold War-era competition for symbolic "firsts."
- This competition involves building orbital data centres, satellite networks, and computing systems to perform data processing directly in space, moving beyond the traditional model of sensing in space and computing on Earth.
- SpaceX is a major US player, with plans for orbital data centres driving demand for materials and components, and an imminent expected IPO to be the world's largest.
- China, viewing space as a critical technological battlefield, is actively developing its commercial space sector. Key initiatives include Zhejiang Lab's "Three-Body Computing Constellation" project, aiming to deploy a thousand-satellite network for space computing.

articles.title: Hong Kong activist Joshua Wong expected to plead guilty in foreign collusion case
articles.summary:
*   Joshua Wong has indicated he will plead guilty to conspiring to collude with foreign forces under Hong Kong's national security law.  
*   The charge, alleged to have occurred between July and November 2020, carries a maximum penalty of life imprisonment.  
*   Wong is already serving a 56-month sentence for subversion related to an unofficial primary election, with an expected release as early as 2027.  
*   The case involves alleged plotting with former lawmaker Nathan Law and others to seek foreign sanctions or hostile actions against Hong Kong and mainland China.

articles.title: Tankers with Iranian oil look to Pakistan for safe harbour as US blockade takes effect
articles.summary:
- Two Iranian oil tankers, the *Rani* and the *Amil*, carrying a combined 1 million barrels of crude, have switched their destination signals to Karachi, Pakistan, in an unusual move.
- This is likely a strategic positioning to wait near a relatively secure location as the U.S. naval blockade of Iranian shipping takes effect, rather than an intention to discharge cargo in Pakistan, which has not imported Iranian crude in at least a decade.
- The vessels are part of Iran's sanctioned "dark fleet" and may be seeking to avoid U.S. naval presence or conduct ship-to-ship transfers, a common practice also seen in holding areas like waters off Malaysia.
- The tankers are linked to opaque ownership structures: the *Rani* is linked to a Panama-based entity and the *Amil* to a Malaysia-based owner, with contact details that are non-functional or absent.

articles.title: Southeast Asian scammers’ new disguise: your leader’s face
articles.summary:
- **High-impact fraud in Singapore**: A scammer used a deepfake video of Prime Minister Lawrence Wong in a fabricated Zoom meeting to defraud a victim of US$3.8 million. The victim was lured via a WhatsApp message and attended the fake summit with other cloned officials.
- **Regional pattern of AI abuse**: In Indonesia, a deepfake of President Prabowo Subianto was used to trick people into paying fake administrative fees for non-existent aid. In the Philippines, a deepfake of President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. endorsing an investment scheme convinced a victim to lose US$1.5 million.
- **Growing criminal capability**: Generative AI is enabling scammers to convincingly impersonate trusted leaders, moving beyond simple forged images to create detailed, interactive fabrications that exploit human trust.
- **Systemic threat**: An Interpol report identified AI-driven crime as a major emerging threat in Southeast Asia, noting that criminal networks are becoming more organized and efficient as technology outpaces regulation.

articles.title: China’s historic sea-based rocket recovery puts spotlight on domestic shipbuilder
articles.summary:
- China achieved the world's first sea-based net recovery of an orbital-class rocket booster, making it only the second nation after the US to perform a controlled recovery of an orbital rocket's first stage.

- The recovery platform, Linghangzhe, was designed and built by Guangzhou Shipyard International (GSI), a subsidiary of China State Shipbuilding Corporation, using a domestically developed net-capture system.

- Unlike SpaceX's landings on deployable legs, the Chinese system uses hooks on the rocket to latch onto a net suspended from the sea platform, which required overcoming unique engineering challenges like structural loads and stability.

- The converted barge is 144 meters long with a 25,000-tonne displacement, features a 36-meter-high capture net, and utilizes a DP-2 dynamic positioning system for precision holding in rough seas.

- GSI completed the design by end of 2024 and delivered the platform in December after an April start, marking a significant step in China's efforts to develop reusable launch technology.

articles.title: Philippine rice output risks 30% collapse as ‘super’ El Nino strengthens
articles.summary:
- A "super" El Nino is strengthening in the Pacific, with a 62% chance of intensifying from October, threatening severe droughts in the Philippines starting in late 2024/early 2025.
- Philippine agricultural authorities warn this could cause a collapse in national rice output exceeding 30%, potentially cutting production by up to 700,000 tonnes.
- This is compounded by high global fertilizer and fuel prices, as well as potential export restrictions from major rice suppliers like Vietnam, Thailand, and India.
- First-quarter 2026 rice production was already down 6.3% year-on-year to 4.4 million tonnes, marking the weakest start to the year since 2020.
- The warning follows historical precedent: a previous super El Nino in 1997-98 triggered a 22% collapse in Philippine rice production.

articles.title: Why is a Trump commission attacking Nietzsche, Foucault and Sartre?
articles.summary:
- President Trump's Religious Liberty Commission released a 224-page draft report in May arguing that American society has become excessively secular, leading to systemic discrimination against religion.

- The report advocates for reinterpreting the separation of church and state and proposes measures to reverse secularisation in education, government, healthcare, and other sectors, including hotlines to report violations of religious liberty.

- It attributes the erosion of religious values to 20th-century European philosophers Friedrich Nietzsche, Michel Foucault, and Jean-Paul Sartre, blaming their ideas of subjective truth and morality for undermining objective religious values.

- The article notes an irony, pointing out that Trump's own documented behaviour—such as his frequent false or misleading statements (over 30,000 during his first term)—aligns with the "post-truth" characteristics the report criticises.

- The commission's effort is framed as a push by the current government and its religious right base to correct perceived secular overreach, with significant implications for US policy on religion and public life.

articles.title: Osaka-led industry group eyes China mission as corporate Japan navigates trade tensions
articles.summary:
- A 1,300-member Osaka-based Japanese business group (Kansai Economic Federation) plans a delegation to Beijing and Xi'an from October 18-23 to meet Chinese officials, aiming to bolster economic ties.
- The visit follows a significant diplomatic dispute in November 2024, where Japan's Prime Minister's comments on Taiwan prompted China to issue travel warnings, halt seafood imports, and ban dual-use exports.
- Bilateral trade between Japan and China reached US$322 billion last year, and the delegation's stated purpose is to reaffirm and develop future business relations.
- Analysts suggest the mission by the historically pragmatic Kansai business community could help ease high political tensions between the two countries.

articles.title: Mainland Chinese firms on Hong Kong-led Central Asia trip eye city listing: John Lee
articles.summary:
*   A Hong Kong-led delegation to Central Asia in June, which included over 40 Hong Kong representatives and 30 mainland Chinese entrepreneurs, resulted in 96 agreements valued at US$1.65 billion with Kazakhstan and Uzbekistan.
*   Many mainland Chinese companies from the delegation are now actively preparing to list on the Hong Kong stock exchange to raise capital for global expansion, as stated by Chief Executive John Lee.
*   Hong Kong's government established a Task Force on Supporting Mainland Enterprises in Going Global, which has already helped over 200 mainland firms set up or expand operations in the city as a launchpad for overseas growth.
*   Chief Executive Lee emphasized that Hong Kong is leveraging its role as a "superconnector" and financial platform to support businesses amid global geopolitical tensions and protectionism, aligning with national strategies like the Belt and Road Initiative and the 15th Five-Year Plan.

articles.title: Should heavy-duty drones be used to rescue China’s flood victims?
articles.summary:
*   China's use of heavy-duty drones for flood rescues in Guangxi highlights a major technological shift from 18 years ago, when the country relied on risky, low-tech methods like blind paratrooper drops after the Wenchuan earthquake.
*   The rescue operations specifically utilized DJI's agricultural (Agras T-100) and logistics (FlyCart FC-200) drone series, which have a maximum payload of 200kg (441lbs), despite manufacturer warnings against transporting people.
*   These drones performed critical functions such as rapid damage surveys, identifying trapped individuals, and directly airlifting residents from hazardous locations like rooftops and stranded vehicles.
*   While valuable for accessing hard-to-reach areas, the drones faced operational challenges including signal issues from damaged communication infrastructure, which affected positioning and control.

articles.title: China used a giant net to land a reusable rocket. Does the idea have legs?
articles.summary:
- China's Long March-10B rocket became the first orbital-class booster outside the U.S. to be recovered intact, using a ship-based giant net with hooks instead of landing legs.

- The method eliminates heavy landing gear, potentially allowing greater payload capacity, but the costs of building and operating the advanced recovery vessel "Linghangzhe" are undisclosed.

- Experts note the ship is "capital intensive" with sophisticated maintenance needs, questioning whether the approach truly reduces launch costs—a core goal of reusability.

- A space historian suggests the system may be cost-effective long-term, leveraging China's shipbuilding strengths, but may be less applicable for future lunar or Martian launches.

- In its reusable configuration, the rocket can deliver 16 tonnes to low-Earth orbit or 11 tonnes to mid-altitude orbits planned for China's satellite mega-constellations.

articles.title: Chinese delivery worker uniform wins top French Design Award
articles.summary:
-   **Award Recognition:** Alibaba's delivery service, Taobao Shangou, won the top Gold Award at a prestigious French design competition for its new delivery worker uniform. This is the first time a uniform has received this honor in the award's 11-year history.
-   **Design and Features:** The uniform is praised for enhancing safety, comfort, and professional dignity, with a "racing-inspired" design. Launched in August 2025, it includes safety features like smart earbuds for navigation and helmets that can detect crashes and share location data for emergencies.
-   **Scale and Context:** The free suits are provided to over one million riders. The design is framed as a response to growing attention on China's gig economy, aiming to improve working conditions and public perception after past scrutiny over rider welfare.
-   **Policy Background:** The article notes this initiative aligns with recent Chinese government efforts, including a State Council plan for better labour protections, social security, and fair wages for gig workers.

articles.title: Hong Kong’s 3-year deal to host LEAP East ‘affirms’ global I&T role
articles.summary:
- Hong Kong hosted the inaugural LEAP East technology exhibition, attracting over 25,000 visitors (55% international) and securing an exclusive 3-year deal to be the event's sole Asian host.
- The event, along with earlier conferences like Consensus Hong Kong and the Web3 Festival, is cited as affirming Hong Kong's status as a global innovation and technology hub and a premier MICE destination.
- Key metrics from the conference included 340+ speakers, 450+ exhibitors, and over 400 investors, featuring discussions on topics like digital assets and Web3.
- Saudi Arabia's consul general highlighted Hong Kong's role as a gateway to mainland China as a primary reason for selecting it to host the flagship event outside the Middle East.
- Organisers and Hong Kong officials position the city as a strategic connector between Middle Eastern technology enterprises and Asian markets, leveraging its status as a "superconnector."

articles.title: Malaysia has never had so many Chinese tourists. It wants more
articles.summary:
*   **Record Growth and Ambitious Targets:** Chinese tourist arrivals in Malaysia rose 25% in 2025 to 4.7 million, and surged another 25% year-on-year in the first quarter of 2026. The country aims to welcome 7 million Chinese visitors in 2026.
*   **Strategic Promotion Campaign:** Malaysia's target is supported by visa-free travel, expanded direct flights to Chinese third-tier cities, and a digital marketing push on mega-apps like Douyin (TikTok) and Weibo.
*   **Competitive Regional Landscape:** Malaysia is benefiting as competitor Thailand cuts its Chinese tourist forecast due to safety and cost concerns, while Vietnam has become the region's top destination for Chinese visitors.
*   **Economic Impact and Appeal:** Foreign tourist receipts in Malaysia increased to 110.6 billion ringgit (US$26.8 billion) in 2025. The country's multicultural society and reputation for safety are key attractions for Chinese travelers.
*   **Traveler Behavior:** Chinese tourists are increasingly exploring beyond traditional hotspots like Kuala Lumpur, using social media platforms to discover and visit lesser-known sites.

articles.title: Your Hong Kong weekend food guide for July 10-12
articles.summary:
*   **New Italian Destination:** Ponentino opens Friday, July 10, in Tseung Kwan O as a new rooftop Italian restaurant. It offers a more relaxed take on the cuisine from the renowned Sabatini, with a tasting menu priced from HK$528 (lunch) to HK$788 (dinner).
*   **Anniversary Celebration:** French bistro Jean-Pierre in Central marks its first anniversary on Saturday, July 11, with a celebratory menu featuring classic dishes like steak tartare and beef bourguignon.
*   **Portuguese Bakery Launch:** Santa Nata opens its first Asian location on Sunday, July 12, on Lyndhurst Terrace in Central, introducing its signature pastel de nata and other Portuguese pastries to Hong Kong.

articles.title: Malaysia’s Anwar warns any Israelis at Johor tech commune to be ‘deported immediately’
articles.summary:
- Malaysian Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim stated that any Israeli nationals found at the tech commune would be "deported immediately," citing Malaysia's non-recognition of Israel.  
- The warning followed a national security investigation into The Network School, a co-living project in Johor's Forest City founded by former Coinbase executive Balaji Srinivasan, after online claims suggested Israeli involvement.  
- Malaysia's Immigration Department inspected 266 foreign nationals from 40 countries at the site and confirmed all held valid documents, but further checks into potential misuse of identities or permits are ongoing.  
- The Network School describes itself as a "frontier community" with membership starting at US$1,500 per month; the Johor state government is also reviewing its premises licenses and operations for compliance.

articles.title: Hong Kong police chief defends plan to deny pay rises to bottom 5% of performers
articles.summary:
• Hong Kong Police Commissioner Joe Chow Yat-ming publicly supports a new civil service appraisal reform that will withhold annual pay increments from the bottom 5% of performers, calling it a fair system that reflects performance standards.

• The reform, part of Chief Executive John Lee's governance efficiency package, has drawn criticism from the Junior Police Officers' Association, which warned it could foster a "shoe-shining culture" and make assessments dependent on personal connections with supervisors.

• Commissioner Chow dismissed the concerns, expressing full confidence in fair evaluations by managers and noting the government stated the 5% target is not rigid. An internal platform has been established for officers to raise concerns.

• In a separate issue, Chow stated that an ongoing police investigation into an embryo samples mix-up at Heal Fertility clinic has not yet found evidence of criminal activity, though the clinic has been ordered to suspend most services.

• The police force has over 20,000 officers in the junior ranks and manages a total strength of approximately 33,000 personnel.

articles.title: Mark Wahlberg savours Penang’s charm as Malaysians floored by star’s humility
articles.summary:
*   Mark Wahlberg interacted casually with locals in Penang, visiting street food stalls and markets while filming for the Netflix crime thriller *The Big Fix*, a visit that went viral and was praised by Malaysians for his down-to-earth demeanor.
*   The production is primarily based in Sydney, but Penang serves as an international filming location, with local sites including Lorong Kulit and City Stadium, under the direction of Baltasar Kormákur.
*   The film stars Wahlberg and Riz Ahmed, with a plot involving a former Interpol officer uncovering a global match-fixing network within FIFA, and features an international cast including actors from Singapore, India, and Malaysia.
*   The project highlights Malaysia's appeal to global productions, which the country supports with a 30% incentive on filming costs, a strategy similar to Thailand's successful film rebate scheme.
*   Local authorities and tourism bodies, including the Penang Island Mayor and Penang Global Tourism, welcomed the production, noting its positive impact on the region's visibility as a filming destination.

articles.title: Hong Kong proposes job-related injury compensation for gig delivery workers
articles.summary:
Here are the key points from the article:

*   Hong Kong has proposed legislation to provide compensation for job-related injuries to gig workers in food and goods delivery services, covering accidents from the start of a job until delivery or specific post-delivery periods.
*   The proposal adopts a "no-fault" principle, meaning platform operators must pay compensation in most cases, even if the worker was negligent, except for deliberately self-inflicted injuries or those involving drugs/alcohol.
*   Compensation would include payments for medical expenses, sick leave, permanent incapacity, and death. Minimum amounts are set at HK$514,510 for death and HK$584,220 for permanent total incapacity, aligning with existing employee standards.
*   Coverage applies during the designated work period (e.g., en route to pick up or deliver, and after delivery), and for commuting accidents within four hours of finishing work during severe weather warnings.
*   The proposed mechanism is limited to the food and goods delivery sectors as they employ the majority of gig workers in Hong Kong, and occupational diseases are explicitly excluded.

articles.title: Why Chinese youth aren’t booing AI, unlike American graduates
articles.summary:
- US university graduates have booed speakers praising AI, reacting not to the technology itself but to its perceived threat to entry-level jobs and career prospects, with recent graduate unemployment and underemployment rates remaining high.
- In contrast, Chinese youth approach AI with more optimism, as the government frames it as a national application project through policies like "AI Plus," while open-source models lower barriers for experimentation.
- The core difference highlighted is a matter of agency: American institutions often ask graduates to absorb AI's consequences, whereas China's policies actively link AI development to creating new employment opportunities.
- The article argues that the US remains a leader in AI research ("zero to one" innovation), but this frontier capability is concentrated in large firms and does not translate into broad economic agency for new graduates.

articles.title: China seen using new tool to finance hi-tech advances amid slower economic growth
articles.summary:
*   China may accelerate deployment of an 800 billion yuan ($117.94 billion) "New Policy-Based Financial Instrument" to support hi-tech industries, including AI supply chains, according to Goldman Sachs.
*   The move is a response to slowing quarterly economic growth and the ongoing US-China technology race, aiming to provide long-term funding to help China achieve tech self-sufficiency.
*   The instrument is designed to meet a dual objective: supporting strategic hi-tech investments while limiting risks to local government debt, offering an alternative to traditional lending channels and older financing vehicles.
*   Goldman Sachs estimates that if the full 800 billion yuan is deployed this quarter, it could add a baseline 0.5 percentage points to GDP through early 2027, acting as a more effective growth multiplier than previous schemes.

articles.title: China has poured funding into flood defences. Has the investment paid off?
articles.summary:
*   The Chinese central government has allocated 44.77 billion yuan ($6.6 billion) in fiscal transfers for water conservancy infrastructure, a level maintained from the previous year.
*   Funding is needed to retrofit ageing dams and reservoirs, as dozens were built over 60 years ago, alongside new projects.
*   160 billion yuan from ultra-long special treasury bonds has been designated for urban flood control, focusing on underground pipeline construction and retrofits. A significant portion of existing drainage pipelines are over 20 years old.
*   Increased investment follows severe recent flood disasters, such as the 2021 Zhengzhou floods (398 deaths/missing, 120 billion yuan in losses), which have highlighted infrastructure weaknesses.

articles.title: Singapore ministers each awarded US$177,860 in damages in Bloomberg defamation suit
articles.summary:
- The High Court in Singapore has awarded two cabinet ministers S$230,000 (US$177,860) each in damages following a defamation trial involving financial news outlet Bloomberg and one of its reporters over the officials’ property transactions.
- Shanmugam, the coordinating minister for national securi...

articles.title: Hongkongers spend record HK$2,000 per month on cats as ‘silver’ feline market grows
articles.summary:
- **Record Monthly Spending**: Hong Kong cat owners now spend an average of HK$2,062 (US$263) per month on their pets, marking a 2.8% year-on-year increase and establishing a new record.

- **"Cat Silver Economy" as Key Driver**: The primary factor behind rising costs is the aging feline population. Cats aged 4-7 years have increased significantly from 20.8% in 2024 to 28.2% this year. This cohort is expected to become seniors within 3-5 years, driving major growth in medical care, prescription food, and longevity-focused products.

- **Market Scale and Spending Shifts**: Total monthly expenditure on pet cats in Hong Kong has surpassed HK$500 million, with the annual figure rising to HK$6.92 billion. While food and litter remain top costs, medical and healthcare products now account for 14.4% of total spending.

- **Household and Breed Trends**: The average owner keeps

articles.title: Hong Kong steps up to US crypto fund challenge with tokenised fund approval
articles.summary:
- Hong Kong's Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) has approved the first "digitally native" tokenized fund (BAGEY) for professional investors, issued by Baillie Gifford. The fund is an actively managed portfolio of short-duration bonds.
- Unlike most tokenized funds that use a "digital twin" model, this fund uses the public blockchain (Ethereum and Solana) as the official ownership record, granting token holders direct legal rights to the underlying assets.
- The approval represents Hong Kong's competitive step against the U.S. in the growing tokenized fund sector, where firms like BlackRock and Franklin Templeton are also active.
- The fund's structure allows trading with fiat currencies or the USDC stablecoin, supporting Hong Kong's broader strategy to establish itself as a global digital asset hub through a completed regulatory framework.

articles.title: China is now world’s No 1 science power, top Russian scientist Artem Oganov says
articles.summary:
- Russian scientist Artem Oganov received China's top international science and technology award, calling China the world's "No 1 science power," a prediction he first made in 2008.
- Oganov cited China's scientific output, noting it accounts for one-third of global publications and holds the top spot in the 2026 Nature Index, surpassing the United States.
- He highlighted that China's rise in scientific leadership has been faster and stronger than expected, occurring alongside its leading positions in industry and economy.
- Oganov stressed the role of science as a "bridge" between cultures during an era of political division, stating the future belongs to bridge-builders.
- A distinguished theoretical crystallographer and developer of the USPEX program, he continues active collaboration with China, visiting annually and planning new projects.

articles.title: Why China is seriously cultivating AI entertainment
articles.summary:
*   China's government is actively promoting AI integration into its entertainment market, projected to reach $441 billion by 2035, through policies encouraging cinemas to become multi-purpose AI hubs for broader cultural consumption.
*   This initiative aims to combat slumping box office revenues by shifting from a ticket-sales model to an immersive, AI-driven consumer experience that includes retail, gaming, and personalized services.
*   The AI short drama and animation sector saw explosive growth in Q1 2025, with AI tools driving nearly 130 billion views on Douyin, demonstrating rapid market scaling and a transformation in content production.
*   AI is lowering barriers to entry and costs, enabling smaller creators and "one-person studios" to compete with established companies, thereby democratizing content production.
*   The strategy is part of China's broader economic focus on stimulating growth through domestic consumption, using AI to increase engagement and spending within the entertainment and cultural economy.

articles.title: Pak Shek Kok station on East Rail line scheduled to open as early as 2033
articles.summary:
- Pak Shek Kok station on Hong Kong's East Rail line is projected to open as early as 2033.
- The project will be financed by allowing the MTR Corporation to build and sell private residential flats on two land parcels near the station.
- The existing Education University of Hong Kong sports ground will be relocated to a new 7.5-hectare site, with land resumption costs estimated at HK$400 million (US$51 million).
- The development includes a footbridge over Tolo Highway to enhance connectivity between the station, residential areas, and the Hong Kong Science and Technology Park.

articles.title: Chinese hard tech giants see value surge in first half as global investors pour in capital
articles.summary:
*   Overseas investment in China's hard-tech sector reached a record 3.13 trillion yuan (US$461.65 billion) via the Stock Connect scheme by the end of Q2, marking a strategic shift from traditional consumer and financial stocks.
*   Technology and advanced manufacturing firms dominate foreign-held A-shares, with the top three positions held by CATL, Zhongji Innolight, and Naura Technology.
*   Chinese equity markets experienced a significant sell-off, with the Shanghai Star 50 Index falling 3.42% and the ChiNext 50 Index dropping around 3%, driven by global tech weakness and geopolitical concerns.
*   Goldman Sachs highlighted a sector rotation favoring onshore "hard-tech" over offshore "soft-tech" platforms and stated that the Chinese AI equity market is not a bubble, suggesting its economic potential is undervalued.

articles.title: How Beijing proposes to solve South China Sea disputes 10 years after Hague ruling
articles.summary:
- On the 10th anniversary of the 2016 Hague tribunal ruling that invalidated most of China's claims in the South China Sea, a 14-country coalition led by the US and Philippines reiterated the decision is "legally binding," while China dismissed it as "null and void."
- China rejects the tribunal's authority, blames US-led military deployments as the "primary threat" to regional peace, and continues to claim historic rights and sovereignty over most of the disputed waters and features.
- Tensions remain high, with recent joint US-Philippine patrols near Scarborough Shoal followed by Chinese combat patrols, and Manila pushing to finalize a code of conduct as the upcoming 2026 ASEAN chair.
- Chinese analysts suggest the unenforced ruling has undermined regional maritime order and propose a shift from focusing on the arbitration to good-faith diplomacy and practical cooperation on issues like marine conservation and fisheries.

articles.title: EU isn’t just selling aircraft to China. It’s helping strengthen a competitor
articles.summary:
*   China Eastern, an operator of China's C919 jet, ordered 25 Airbus A330neo aircraft for $9.35 billion, despite its government's goal to supplant foreign suppliers. This is seen as paying for operational knowledge and supply-chain integration.
*   The EU-Airbus relationship is used as leverage by Beijing. An "administrative delay" by China's aviation regulator withheld approvals for ~$5.7 billion in completed Airbus aircraft, impacting deliveries and applying pressure on the EU.
*   Airbus has deep operational ties in China, holding a 55% market share and operating a final assembly line in Tianjin that has produced over 780 aircraft since 2008.
*   Technology transfer extends into aircraft operations, exemplified by a Sino-foreign joint venture becoming the first facility in China authorized to maintain the C919's LEAP-1C engines.
*   These transactions align with China's strategic goal, stated in its five-year plan, to achieve self-sufficiency in critical supply chains like commercial aircraft.

articles.title: 125 arrested as triads use violence to take over construction site lunchbox trade
articles.summary:
*   Hong Kong police arrested 125 people in an undercover operation targeting a triad syndicate that monopolized the lunchbox supply trade at East Kowloon construction sites through violence, extortion, and intimidation.
*   The syndicate, selling about 800 lunchboxes daily at HK$50 each, had an annual turnover of nearly HK$12 million and was found to have laundered approximately HK$64 million in suspected illicit proceeds between 2023 and 2024.
*   Arrests included the alleged mastermind, key members, and employees, with charges ranging from triad-related offenses and extortion to money laundering and operating unlicensed food facilities.
*   Police seized evidence valued at HK$4 million, including cash, luxury watches, vehicles, and gambling paraphernalia, and concurrently raided four illegal gambling dens in West Kowloon.

articles.title: Chinese medical device makers push into Europe as anti-corruption squeeze bites at home
articles.summary:
• Chinese medical device purchases by public hospitals fell approximately 12% year-on-year in the first five months of 2026, driven partly by an intensified anti-corruption probe, squeezing revenues for domestic makers.

• Companies like Ningbo-based Jenscare Scientific are accelerating European expansion; its LuX-Valve Plus heart valve received EU CE certification, marking a milestone for its international strategy.

• European governments, such as Germany, are increasingly receptive to cost-effective Chinese devices due to fiscal pressures and healthcare cost-saving measures, though prices for these devices are often higher overseas than in China due to intense domestic competition.

• The ongoing anti-corruption campaign in China, launched in 2023, targets kickbacks and improper incentives in hospital procurement, contributing to market pressures that push firms to seek growth abroad.

• Major Chinese device makers like Mindray and Snibe have faced lowered earnings estimates, reflecting the impact of the domestic market squeeze on their financial performance.

articles.title: Dollar risks rising but Plaza Accord-style coordination not feasible now: ex-IMF adviser
articles.summary:
*   Former IMF adviser Barry Eichengreen states that a coordinated, Plaza Accord-style intervention to manage the dollar's value is not feasible in the 21st century.
*   He attributes this to central banks being primarily mandated to pursue domestic objectives (like price stability and employment), leaving little scope for large-scale international coordination.
*   Eichengreen argues that risks to the dollar-centered system—including US fiscal debt, threats to Fed independence, and tariffs—are eroding confidence, making a disorderly loss of confidence in the dollar more likely.
*   He outlines two possible futures: a gradual shift to a multipolar monetary system or an abrupt crisis of confidence before alternatives are ready, with the latter's probability exceeding 50% if current erratic policies continue for another decade.
*   The transition to a multipolar system, while desirable, would be difficult to coordinate, and stability requires a very gradual shift.

articles.title: Dashcam row allegedly involving singer David Lui could be referred to police
articles.summary:
* Hong Kong's privacy watchdog received a complaint about a viral dashcam video showing a dispute between a driver and a passenger, identified as singer David Lui. The driver uploaded the identifiable footage to social media without consent.
* The Assistant Privacy Commissioner stated that the driver violated data protection principles, as for-hire vehicles are considered semi-private spaces with a high expectation of privacy. The watchdog ordered the clip's removal.
* The case could be referred to police for potential legal follow-up. Sharing personal data without consent, especially with intent or recklessness causing harm, carries a penalty of up to two years' imprisonment and a HK$100,000 fine under Hong Kong's anti-doxxing law.
* Authorities emphasized that professional drivers must follow guidelines, including displaying clear signage, when installing dashcams in vehicles to protect passenger privacy.

articles.title: Macau intensifies fight against single-use plastic, aiming for near-zero emissions by 2050
articles.summary:
• Macau banned the import of non-degradable styrofoam trays, plastic cups, and plates starting in 2024, building on previous restrictions on plastic cutlery, straws, and other items since 2019.  
• According to 2022 data, Macau residents generate 1.77 kg of solid waste per day—higher than Hong Kong and more than double that of Guangzhou.  
• Plastic products accounted for 23.5% of the 550,249 tonnes of municipal solid waste collected in 2019, making it the second-largest waste category after organic waste.  
• A resurgence in tourism has driven waste volumes back toward pre-pandemic levels, with municipal solid waste increasing nearly 15% in 2023 compared to 2022.  
• The measures support Macau’s long-term environmental goals, including achieving near-zero emissions by 2050.

articles.title: Sexual offences law review a chance to make clear what constitutes abuse
articles.summary:
- Hong Kong's government is reviewing sexual offences law to address inadequacies exposed by modern forms of sexual violence, harassment, and online exploitation.
- The proposed reforms aim to legally define "consent" and establish a gender-equal framework that focuses on free and voluntary agreement, moving beyond outdated notions like the absence of resistance.
- A key goal is to strengthen protections for vulnerable groups, including children under 16, people with mental impairment, and marginalized communities.
- This review is the product of over a decade of work by the Law Reform Commission, not a reaction to recent events, with formal studies and consultations conducted since 2006.
- Hong Kong's current laws are largely based on the 1956 UK Sexual Offences Act; the reform seeks to align with modern standards set by jurisdictions like England, Canada, and Australia.

articles.title: Chinese gem lab rakes in profits as world embraces artificial diamonds
articles.summary:
*   Chinese lab-grown diamond producer Power Diamond projects first-half 2026 profits to nearly triple year-on-year, reaching up to 93 million yuan, with revenues forecast to double to 450 million yuan due to process breakthroughs and export growth.
*   China is poised to dominate the global market, expected to account for nearly two-thirds (65%) of global lab-grown diamond output by 2030, with most production concentrated in Henan province.
*   The rise of man-made diamonds has significantly impacted natural diamond demand; natural diamond prices have fallen 30% over three years without recovery, prompting industry players like De Beers to cut costs and suspend production.
*   The global lab-grown diamond market is valued at approximately 127.2 billion yuan (US$18.8 billion), with the United States representing the greatest consumer demand.

articles.title: Connect scheme for gold shines light on China’s financial liberalisation
articles.summary:
- China is pursuing a distinctive, gradual approach to financial liberalization through carefully managed cross-border "Connect" schemes, rather than wholesale deregulation and rapid capital account opening.
- The Connect program has evolved from initial stock and bond channels (Stock Connect 2014, Bond Connect 2017) to encompass wealth management, derivatives, and now physical commodities.
- The newest "Delivery Connect" establishes a gold clearing and settlement system between Hong Kong and mainland China, extending the model into the physical commodities market.
- This strategy uses Hong Kong as a testing ground and bridge to integrate with global financial markets while maintaining oversight of capital flows and risks.
- The expansion into gold infrastructure supports Hong Kong's ambition to become a leading international hub for gold trading and settlement, amid growing global demand for gold as a strategic asset.

articles.title: As consumption worries persist, Hong Kong investors shun mainland Chinese F&B stocks
articles.summary:
*   Hong Kong investors are avoiding mainland Chinese F&B stocks due to persistent worries about weak consumption, leading to failed or delayed IPO applications for several major brands in 2026.
*   Listed Chinese consumer stocks underperformed significantly, with a Goldman Sachs tracker falling an average of 17% in Q2 2026, despite strong revenue growth at companies like Mixue Group.
*   Market caution is fueled by weak retail sales, labour market pressures from AI adoption, and adverse weather; China's May retail sales shrank 0.6% year-on-year for the first time in over three years.
*   The State Council has approved a five-year consumption plan targeting annual retail sales of ~60 trillion yuan by 2030, implying a slowdown in growth to about 3.66% annually.
*   Increased regulatory scrutiny by Chinese authorities on red-chip listings in Hong Kong is adding uncertainty to the IPO timeline for mainland companies.

articles.title: China’s No 4 official Wang Huning to head to North Korea as visits multiply
articles.summary:
*   **High-Level Chinese Visit:** China's fourth-ranked official, Wang Huning, will lead a delegation on an "official goodwill visit" to North Korea from Wednesday to Friday.
*   **Cycle of Exchanges:** The trip follows North Korean Premier Pak Thae-song's recent visit to Beijing and is the third senior Chinese official's visit to Pyongyang in three months, after President Xi Jinping in June and top diplomat Wang Yi in April.
*   **Strategic Context:** Chinese President Xi Jinping recently called on both nations to expand cooperation, maintain "firm strategic resolve," and defend their sovereignty and security interests.
*   **Analysis of Warming Ties:** An analyst stated the visit shows China-North Korea relations have entered a phase of "continuous, institutionalised advancement," not just a one-off event.
*   **Regional Motivations:** The warming relationship is seen as a strategic move by both sides to reaffirm ties amid a complex security situation in Northeast Asia, including strengthened US-Japan-South Korea cooperation.

articles.title: Singapore-Malaysia relations enter ‘mature’ era with Tharman’s state visit
articles.summary:
*   **Diplomatic milestone:** Singapore President Tharman Shanmugaratnam is making his first state visit to Malaysia, reciprocating a visit by Malaysia's King in May 2024, which analysts cite as a sign of strengthening ties and a more "mature" relationship.
*   **Strong economic integration:** Singapore is Malaysia's second-largest trading partner, with bilateral trade reaching 402.35 billion ringgit (US$98 billion) in 2025, and about 120,000 Singaporeans live and work in Malaysia.
*   **Moving past historical disputes:** Analysts

articles.title: How the next China shock is shaping hearts and minds
articles.summary:
*   China is transitioning from being primarily a low-cost global manufacturer to a driver of higher-value innovation, brands, and cultural trends, particularly in sectors like smartphones, EVs, and digital services.
*   The "next China shock" is defined by cultural and consumer influence (termed "Created in China") through global successes like the game *Black Myth: Wukong*, Labubu, micro dramas, and boba tea chains, which may have long-term geopolitical significance.
*   China's competitive edge is evolving from low labor costs to unmatched supply chain depth, industrial coordination, and an innovation ecosystem capable of rapid product iteration and scaling.
*   Digital commerce and platform distribution form the critical "nervous system" supporting this shift, enabling the global spread of Chinese brands and cultural exports as tools of soft power.
*   This represents a geopolitical competition extending beyond trade and technology into the realm of cultural imagination and brand storytelling.

articles.title: Malaysia’s Anwar deals with another split with BN in Melaka after Johor poll rout
articles.summary:
-   Melaka's state assembly passed a constitutional amendment allowing the appointment of up to seven unelected assembly members with voting rights, a move proposed by BN and opposed by PH.
-   In protest, PH's five state lawmakers (four from DAP and one from Amanah) resigned from their government posts and moved to the opposition bench, though this does not threaten the BN-led government's majority.
-   Analysts state the split reveals the fraying "spirit of power-sharing" in the unity government across Malaysian states, even as the federal coalition remains intact.
-   The conflict centers on a democratic principle; PH argues unelected members voting breaches core electoral values, while BN contends it would widen representation for various groups.
-   The rupture occurs amid BN's recent electoral ascendancy in Johor and precedes upcoming elections in Melaka and Negeri Sembilan, testing the stability of the federal alliance at the state level.

articles.title: Hong Kong’s war-risk pool testament to collaboration, insurance leader says
articles.summary:
- The launch of a marine war-risk pool by Hong Kong insurers is a prime example of how the city’s insurers can collaborate not just for the benefit of the local economy but for the entire nation, an industry veteran has said.
- At the South China Morning Post’s China Conference, themed “Intelligence...

articles.title: Why a Chinese academic claim over Batanes has unsettled the Philippines
articles.summary:
- A university symposium in Guangzhou has placed the Philippines’ northernmost province of Batanes at the centre of a new argument over history, geography and sovereignty, after scholars at the event claimed the islands belonged to Taiwan and, on that basis, fell under overall Chinese sovereignty.
-...

articles.title: China’s missile test reveals fragile state of world nuclear governance
articles.summary:
*   A Chinese submarine-launched ballistic missile test on July 6 prompted immediate regional concerns from the U.S., Australia, Japan, and Pacific nations over insufficient notification and its implications for Pacific nuclear-free norms.
*   The test is analyzed as evidence of China's maturing sea-based second-strike capability, a challenge to U.S. allies, and a signal to states reconsidering their nuclear policies, revealing a nuclear order in transition.
*   Global nuclear governance is increasingly structured around three tracks: a hardening Sino-Russian strategic alignment (cooperating to challenge U.S. missile defense and alliances), a hardening U.S.-led extended deterrence system (involving allies like South Korea and Japan), and a fluid middle layer of secondary nuclear powers and threshold states.
*   China's test bolsters the credibility of its nuclear modernization as defensive and strengthens the Sino-Russian narrative that "strategic stability" must address U.S. alliances and advanced conventional weapons.
*   The U.S

articles.title: Chinese AI labs to challenge Thinking Machines Lab with new industry focus
articles.summary:
- Two former senior executives from major Chinese AI companies—Zhang Fan (ex-Zhipu AI) and Yang Hongxia (ex-ByteDance/Alibaba)—have launched new startups, Yoolee AI and InfiX.ai, respectively, aimed at developing industry-specific AI solutions rather than competing in the general-purpose AI race.
- Their ventures explicitly position themselves as alternatives to the US-based Thinking Machines Lab, founded by ex-OpenAI executive Mira Murati, emphasizing practical applications over the pursuit of "all-rounder" general intelligence.
- Yoolee AI focuses on creating self-evolving AI agents for sectors like healthcare and travel, aiming to generate new business revenue by automating tasks too costly for humans. The company has secured backing from venture capital firms including Lanchi Ventures.
- InfiX.ai leverages low-bit training technologies (FP8, FP4) to significantly reduce computational costs, enabling organizations to train specialized models more affordably.
- This shift reflects a broader industry trend where AI leaders are moving away from resource-intensive frontier model development to prioritize solutions that deliver tangible business value for enterprises.

articles.title: Xi Jinping to attend World AI Conference for first time as China elevates tech push
articles.summary:
*   President Xi Jinping will personally attend the 2026 World AI Conference in Shanghai, marking his first in-person appearance at the event since its 2018 launch, which signals AI's elevated priority in China's economic and strategic plans.
*   The conference will be held alongside a high-level global AI governance meeting and is expected to feature over 1,400 guests, 1,100 exhibitors, and the debut of more than 300 new products.
*   China's policy focus on AI includes expanding its "AI+" campaign, accelerating commercial adoption, and shaping international rules through initiatives like the Global AI Governance Initiative proposed in 2023.
*   Domestic Chinese AI models are closing the performance gap with U.S. rivals; an OpenRouter platform report noted Chinese models surpassed U.S. models in token share in early June, and a Stanford study found the performance gap narrowed to 2.7% by March 2026.

articles.title: Mujun Planet: one man’s quest to revive Japan’s artisanal legacy
articles.summary:
- **Founder and Project**: Shinya Kobayashi, a 39-year-old designer and artist, established "Mujun Planet" on a mountain in Shimane prefecture, Japan, a self-sustaining artisanal village built over six years to address the crisis of craftsman succession.
- **Facilities and Focus**: The site includes a blacksmith's forge, sawmill, woodworking shop, and charcoal kilns, with initial emphasis on metalworking. Knives forged there are sold globally through his distribution company, Coelacanth Shokudo.
- **Operational Model**: The project aims for self-sufficiency by using local raw materials, such as iron sand and charcoal from nearby Yunotsu for blades, and locally harvested wood for handles, reducing reliance on imported tools.
- **Broader Context**: Kobayashi's work responds to the accelerating decline in

articles.title: Hong Kong woman gets 10 years in jail for luring victims into gang’s extortion plot
articles.summary:
- A Hong Kong woman has been sentenced to 10 years in prison for assisting in robbing three people when she was a teenager by luring them to a deserted location before her fellow gang members attacked them and took nude photos for extortion.
- The High Court sentenced 23-year-old Hau Yan-ki on Tuesd...

articles.title: Iran war is no longer a contest over just the Strait of Hormuz
articles.summary:
- Early on July 12, after Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz closed until further notice and struck a Cyprus-flagged container ship, the United States launched its third round of strikes in a week, hitting some 140 targets.
- Most were along Iran’s southern coast overlooking the strait – including B...

articles.title: As IPOs surge, Hong Kong’s audit regulator vows tougher scrutiny
articles.summary:
- Hong Kong's audit regulator (AFRC) will prioritize inspections of newly listed companies amid a surge in IPOs, focusing on firms that listed or will list within the past year.
- IPO activity in Hong Kong has doubled since late 2025, with over 80 new listings completed in the first half of the year and a pipeline of more than 430 applicants awaiting approval.
- In the previous financial year, the AFRC imposed over HK$11.7 million in fines and identified 17 cases of misconduct linked to audits of public or listed companies.
- The regulator issued a warning to auditors not to sacrifice reporting quality for business growth, emphasizing key areas for improvement such as revenue recognition and fraud risk assessment.
- Despite the tougher stance, the AFRC noted that overall audit quality has improved and that its oversight has not identified systemic failures to date.

articles.title: Markets, not politics, will make China’s yuan a global currency
articles.summary:
- The yuan's internationalization faces significant market-based challenges, as practical use by global financial institutions and corporations depends on predictable funding, hedging, and exit costs, not just political promotion.
- While China has made progress, the yuan remains a small player in global finance: it accounted for only 2.75% of international payments and 1.99% of global reserves, with the US dollar still overwhelmingly dominant.
- Key barriers include insufficient offshore liquidity and the high cost of direct currency conversion, forcing many transactions to route through the US dollar and limiting the yuan's independence.
- True currency power requires financing projects and anchoring markets, not just settling trade; the yuan has achieved the latter but not the former, requiring deeper offshore funding markets and more stable liquidity.

articles.title: Tourist arrivals in Hong Kong jump by 13% to 26.7 million in first half of year
articles.summary:
*   Hong Kong tourist arrivals rose 13% year-on-year to 26.71 million in the first half of 2026, driven by increased mega-events and expanded flight destinations.
*   Visitor spending shows a structural shift from shopping to culture and experiences, with per capita spending by overnight visitors forecast to rise slightly to HK$5,530 in 2026.
*   Arrivals from mainland China increased by 16%, while foreign tourist arrivals grew by 5%.
*   The government's "mega-events plus tourism" strategy is credited with extending visitor stays and broadening consumption, exemplified by Kai Tak Sports Park hosting over 60 major events since its 2025 opening.
*   The Airport Authority is using financial incentives to attract airlines to add new destinations and increase flight frequencies to support tourism growth.

articles.title: How China’s aerospace vision differs from Elon Musk’s SpaceX
articles.summary:
*   China successfully recovered an orbital-class rocket booster using a net-capture vessel, a technological milestone achieved through its state-led aerospace program.
*   The article contrasts China's "national team" model, where the state directs strategy and resources, with the U.S. market-driven approach led by private companies like SpaceX.
*   China's method shifts engineering complexity from the rocket (which lacks heavy landing legs) to the recovery ship, representing a distinct technical path.
*   Chinese policymakers view space as a strategic domain for sovereignty and security, believing their state-led system is more resilient and better suited for closing the gap with the U.S.

articles.title: China’s trade surges in first half of 2026, maintaining growth amid global tensions
articles.summary:
*   **Robust growth in June 2026:** China's exports surged 27% year-on-year to $412.39 billion, significantly beating economist forecasts. Imports also grew strongly by 36% to $286.76 billion, resulting in a trade surplus of $125.62 billion.
*   **Key driver and sectoral strength:** The growth was primarily fueled by a boom in technology exports, including AI products and robotics, showcasing the competitiveness of China's manufacturing sector.
*   **Strong performance in H1 2026:** For the first half of the year, exports grew 17.6% and imports increased 26.6%, with officials expressing confidence in maintaining positive momentum despite potential global headwinds.
*   **Regional export trends:** Exports to the United States rose 13.9%, to ASEAN by 34.6%, and to the EU by 18.5% year-on-year in June.
*   **Geopolitical context and analysis:** The strong export performance occurred ahead of a potential U.S. tariff policy change. Economists noted the data may reflect front-loading of shipments and highlighted that continued strong exports could contribute to trade tensions, particularly with Europe.

articles.title: Shein executive chairman to step down as IPO nears completion, sources say
articles.summary:
*   Donald Tang, executive chairman of Shein, will step down from his role and transition to a senior advisory position as the company's IPO process nears completion.
*   Tang served as the public face and Western representative for Shein's secretive founder, Sky Xu, for three years, managing lobbying, regulatory issues, and public appearances.
*   His tenure included leading Shein's failed IPO attempts in New York and London before the company pivoted to a listing in Hong Kong, as well as efforts to improve regulatory compliance following fines.
*   The move raises questions about future leadership, with founder Sky Xu expected to lead the investor roadshow ahead of the listing.

articles.title: Hong Kong’s Paul Chan to make second trip to Saudi Arabia to build Mideast ties
articles.summary:
- Hong Kong’s Financial Secretary Paul Chan will lead a business delegation to Saudi Arabia later this year, focusing on infrastructure, green tech, healthcare, and finance. This marks his second trip following his first official visit in October 2023.
- Hong Kong hosted the first overseas edition of Saudi Arabia’s LEAP technology conference, attracting about 35,000 participants from over 30 countries. The event will continue annually in Hong Kong for the next three years.
- Key areas of collaboration identified include fintech, artificial intelligence, smart city development, and sustainability. Capital markets are already linked via cross-listed ETFs.
- Saudi officials highlighted Hong Kong’s role as a gateway to mainland China and noted growing bilateral ties since Chief Executive John Lee’s 2023 visit.
- The conference featured over 340 speakers, 450 exhibitors, and more than 400 investors, underscoring Hong Kong’s positioning as an international fundraising and innovation hub.

articles.title: US Anthropic ban is best advert for Chinese AI
articles.summary:
- Bankers working for JPMorgan Chase and Goldman Sachs in Hong Kong must have been miffed when they were shut off from using artificial intelligence (AI) models from Anthropic, a pioneering American firm in the field.
- Goldman Sachs and JPMorgan Chase pulled the plug in April and last week respecti...

articles.title: Liu Qian on Chinese innovation, the US rivalry and women holding up half the sky
articles.summary:
- **Broad Scope of US-China Rivalry**: Liu Qian states the competition extends beyond trade into technology, ideology, and global collaboration, warning that continued unhealthy rivalry would harm both nations and the world.
